content: We all experience the changing seasons—spring, summer, fall, and winter—but what causes these shifts? The answer lies in how Earth moves around the sun. As Earth travels in its yearly orbit, it’s not standing straight up; instead, it’s tilted slightly, like a spinning top leaning to one side. This tilt means that during different parts of the year, either the northern or southern half of Earth leans closer to the sun. When your region leans toward the sun, it receives more direct sunlight and longer daylight hours, making it warmer—this is summer. When it leans away, sunlight is less direct and days are shorter, leading to cooler temperatures and winter. This simple tilt and orbit combination creates the familiar cycle of seasons, influencing everything from weather patterns to energy needs and even business planning. Understanding this natural rhythm helps us better anticipate changes in our environment and economy throughout the year.
